crossCuttingSignals.summary: Review heuristic labels before spending proof or eval budget.
Refresh or recheck stale evidence before consuming it as proof.

primaryBuckets.summary: Proof is already attached and valid under packet semantics.
Add or connect unit, lint, build, schema, spec, or CI proof.
Draft or select Cautilus eval scenarios for ready eval claims.
Decompose the behavior into a concrete scenario before protected eval planning.
Reconcile conflicting docs, code, adapters, or ownership boundaries before proof would be honest.
Confirm, decompose, or accept a human-auditable claim before treating it as proven.
Split broad, historical, provider-caveated, policy-like, or otherwise blocked claims before verification.

discoveryBoundary.agentEscapeHatch: Agent-led quality or narrative review may explore the codebase for missing public claims, then record in-scope discovery bugs or out-of-scope alignment and documentation work before expecting deterministic discovery.
discoveryBoundary.omissionPolicy: Claims not declared in configured entry documents or linked Markdown are outside deterministic discovery scope.
discoveryBoundary.productSignal: A declared in-scope promise missed by discovery is a discover claims bug; an important behavior outside the scan boundary is a narrative or catalog gap.

heuristics.summary: Read Markdown headings, bullets, numbered items, and sentence paragraphs outside code fences and frontmatter.
Keep lines that look like behavior promises and drop prompt examples, open questions, definition labels, future placeholders, and very short or long text.
Label the candidate with the kind of follow-up work Cautilus recommends next.
Merge identical normalized candidate text into one candidate with all source references preserved.

satisfiedClaims.summary: `npm run test:on-demand` currently owns the heavier self-dogfood workflow script tests.
Ships as a standalone binary plus Cautilus Agent, which a host repo can install without copying another scaffold first.
Commands should emit durable packets with enough state for the next agent to resume, not only terminal prose for a human operator.
`Cautilus` installs as a machine-level binary, but its agent-facing surface is intentionally repo-local.
They stay checked into each host repo so evaluation behavior remains reproducible, reviewable, and owned by the repo that declares it.
Claude and Codex use the repo-local Cautilus Agent that `cautilus init` installs under `.agents/skills/cautilus-agent/`.
`Cautilus` stays portable by loading repo-specific evaluation commands from an adapter instead of hardcoding benchmark runners into the workflow.
When an eval run uses `runtime=fixture`, observed proof is downgraded to `fixture-smoke` and the adapter-declared class is preserved only as declared metadata.
`kind: explicit` keeps fixture-backed repos and simple single-instance adopters cheap without forcing a probe script.
The command writes one `cautilus.live_run_invocation_result.v1` packet to `output_file`.
`cautilus doctor --next-action` returning the smallest honest next step while wiring remains incomplete
`cautilus --version` must work on `PATH` before any consumer adapter or skill wiring is treated as valid.
If the repo intentionally keeps only named adapters under `.agents/cautilus-adapters/`, use `cautilus doctor --adapter-name <name>` for repo-scope validation.
`cautilus doctor --scope repo` requires the target directory to be a git repository with at least one commit.
The schema version stays at `v1`, but some surface strings have been renamed for legacy vocabulary hygiene.
When repeated workflow failures should become durable scenario coverage, prefer the checked-in `skill` normalization helper over repo-local one-off shapers:
Use a checked-in JSON schema file and a fixed output file path so the loop can detect `blocker`, `concern`, and `pass` without guessing.
`Cautilus` pins one product-owned per-run workspace root per workflow and keeps the reference sticky across consumer command invocations with a shell environment variable.
Review packets and machine-readable curation artifacts should preserve absorbed raw claim ids and fingerprints when available.
When a repo maintains canonical catalogs, it should also keep a machine-readable mapping artifact that projects raw claim ids onto canonical user-facing or maintainer-facing claim ids.
The preflight should never assert that a claim is satisfied solely because a filename looks related.
For each kept scenario, the command expands `samplesPerScenario` into deterministic request ids and emits the canonical batch request packet.
The command writes the aggregated batch packet to `--output-file`.
When the product-owned loop is active, `Cautilus` also owns one stable per-request workspace directory under `<output_file>.d/workspace/` and may run one optional prepare hook before the first turn.
Product-owned workspace lifecycle stays off the public packet shape and instead flows through adapter template placeholders.
For `persona_prompt`, the product owns the loop boundary, the request packet, the persona prompt shaping, and the result normalization.
The adapter still owns backend selection and provider-specific flags through the simulator command template.
The consumer can return a machine-readable result without exposing its route layout.
This keeps report assembly deterministic.
`eval` runs bounded behavior evaluations through adapter-declared runners.
It should combine binary health, skill surface readiness, adapter state, claim state, and runner readiness status.

Top-level `nextBranches` should expose safe orientation branches, including adapter setup, claim refresh, runner assessment, declared runner smoke, claim-map inspection, and stop, without promoting eval execution.
Each branch should expose a stable id, human label, blocking reason, required command or artifact, owning product family or setup helper, and whether the branch writes files.
The observed packet should distinguish fixture-backed smoke, coding-agent messaging, in-process product runner, and live/server product runner.
The shipped schema uses `id`, `surfaces`, `proof_class`, `command_template`, optional `smoke_command_template`, optional `assessment_path`, and optional `default_runtime`.
It should not duplicate the command catalog.
Proof class must remain visible in downstream summaries and reports.
Those proof classes can support setup checks, fixture shaping, and deterministic smoke confidence without claiming app product-path proof.
Every instance must expose a stable `instanceId` and a human-facing `displayLabel`.
The command should print only `cautilus.workbench_instance_catalog.v1` JSON to stdout.
The product can read scenario-adjacent paths from typed packet fields instead of hardcoded route templates.
Those helpers should sit between source-port ingestion and proposal packet generation.
Evidence should preserve where the suggestion came from without forcing one host repo's storage model on the product.
The canonical machine-readable output should preserve the full ranked proposal list.

changedFilesBasis.meaning: changedFiles lists files committed between packetGitCommit and currentGitCommit; it does not include unstaged, staged, or untracked working-tree files.

gitState.recommendedAction: The claim packet commit matches the inspected checkout.

gitStateSnapshotNotice: gitState is computed when this status packet is generated; rerun discover claims status for live checkout state.
nonVerdictNotice: Discovery creates candidates. A discovered claim is not verified until matching evidence is attached.

recommendedNextActions.summary: Prepare bounded LLM claim review clusters after setting a review budget, before drafting eval scenarios.
Add or connect unit, lint, build, schema, or CI proof for deterministic claims.
Reconcile alignment-work claims before treating them as verification targets.

sampleClaims.nextAction: Split this principle into concrete checkable subclaims, such as specific CLI behavior covered by tests, adapter schema rules, specdown assertions, or script-owned validation gates.
sampleClaims.summary: Deterministic behavior belongs in code, scripts, adapters, tests, and specs.
